questions.to_find: Which of the given sets S1, S2, S3, S4 are uncountable

understand.plain: We have four sets of languages/programs. A set is countable if its elements can be listed one by one (mapped to natural numbers); otherwise it is uncountable. Recursively enumerable languages and valid C programs can be enumerated by their Turing machines or grammars (finite descriptions), so they are countable. All languages over {0,1} form the power set of all strings, which is uncountable. Non-regular languages are a subset of all languages that is still uncountable.

keywords.explain: A set whose elements can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with the natural numbers (or is finite).
keywords.example: The set of all finite strings over {0,1} is countably infinite.

keywords.explain: A set too large to be listed by any enumeration; its cardinality exceeds that of the natural numbers.
keywords.example: The set of all real numbers in [0,1] is uncountable (Cantor's diagonal argument).

keywords.explain: A language accepted by some Turing machine; each TM is described by a finite string, so RE languages are countable.
keywords.example: The halting language is RE but not recursive.

keywords.explain: The set of all subsets of a given set. If the base set is countably infinite, its power set is uncountable.
keywords.example: P({0,1}*) = set of all languages over {0,1} is uncountable.

understand.visual_alt: Four sets classified: S1 (RE languages) and S2 (valid C programs) are countable because they have finite descriptions; S3 (all languages over {0,1}) is uncountable as it equals the power set of {0,1}*; S4 (non-regular languages) is an uncountable subset of S3.

given.aim: Identify which of S1, S2, S3, S4 are uncountable sets.

terms.meaning: Every RE language has a Turing machine; Turing machines are finite-length strings, so they are enumerable.
terms.example: TM descriptions can be listed as binary strings.
terms.connects: S1 is countable.

terms.meaning: Each valid C program is a finite string over a finite alphabet; the set of all finite strings over a finite alphabet is countably infinite.
terms.example: Programs can be ordered lexicographically.
terms.connects: S2 is countable.

terms.meaning: This is the power set of {0,1}*, which is countably infinite. By Cantor's theorem, P(countably infinite set) is uncountable.
terms.example: For each infinite binary string we get a distinct language; uncountably many such strings exist.
terms.connects: S3 is uncountable.

terms.meaning: Regular languages are countable (finitely many DFAs up to each size); non-regular languages = S3 minus a countable set = still uncountable.
terms.example: {a^n b^n | n>=0} is one non-regular language; there are uncountably many.
terms.connects: S4 is uncountable.

given.plan: Check each set: if its elements can be described by finite strings (programs/TMs), it is countable. If it is essentially a power set of a countably infinite set, it is uncountable.

steps.title: Check S1 and S2 for countability
steps.apply: Every RE language corresponds to at least one Turing machine. TM descriptions are finite binary strings, so there are at most countably many RE languages. Similarly, valid C programs are finite strings over a finite alphabet, so S2 is countable. Both S1 and S2 are countable.
steps.note: A finite description always implies countability.

steps.title: Check S3 for countability
steps.apply: S3 is the set of ALL languages over {0,1}, i.e., all subsets of {0,1}*. Since {0,1}* is countably infinite, its power set has cardinality 2^(aleph_0), which is uncountable by Cantor's theorem.
steps.note: This is the standard argument: the power set of a countably infinite set is uncountable.

steps.title: Check S4 for countability
steps.formula_raw: S4 = S3 \ {regular languages}; |S3| uncountable, |regular| countable => |S4| uncountable
steps.apply: The set of regular languages is countable (each regular language is described by a finite DFA). Removing a countable set from an uncountable set leaves an uncountable set. Therefore S4 is uncountable.

steps.title: Select answer
steps.apply: S3 and S4 are uncountable; S1 and S2 are countable. The answer is option B.
steps.note: A common trap is thinking non-regular languages might be 'fewer' — they are still uncountable.

solution.result: S3 and S4 are uncountable. Answer: B. S3 and S4

questions.formula_note: Cantor's theorem: the power set of a countably infinite set is uncountable; removing a countable subset from an uncountable set leaves an uncountable set.

questions.question:
State whether the following statement is TRUE or FALSE:
A language L is recursive if both L and its complement are accepted by Turing machines.
A. TRUE
B. FALSE
questions.to_find: Whether the statement 'L is recursive if both L and its complement are RE' is true or false

understand.plain: A language is recursive (decidable) if a Turing machine always halts and correctly accepts or rejects every input. If both L and its complement are recursively enumerable (accepted by some TM, though the TM may not halt on all inputs), then together they cover all inputs, which forces a decision procedure to exist. This is a classical result in computability theory.

keywords.explain: A language for which there is a TM that always halts (accepts or rejects every input); also called decidable.
keywords.example: The set of all palindromes over {a,b} is recursive.

keywords.explain: A language accepted by a TM that may loop forever on inputs not in the language.
keywords.example: The halting problem language is RE but not recursive.

keywords.explain: The set of all strings NOT in L; if L is over alphabet Sigma, complement(L) = Sigma* \ L.
keywords.example: If L = {a^n b^n}, complement(L) contains all strings that are NOT of the form a^n b^n.

understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram: RE languages contain recursive languages; if both L and complement(L) are in RE, then L must be in the recursive (decidable) subset.

given.aim: Verify the classical theorem: L is recursive iff both L and its complement are RE.

terms.meaning: There exists a TM M1 that accepts every string in L (may loop on strings not in L).
terms.example: M1 halts and accepts on 'aabb' if L = {a^n b^n}.
terms.connects: Provides a semi-decision procedure for membership in L.

terms.meaning: There exists a TM M2 that accepts every string NOT in L.
terms.example: M2 halts and accepts on 'ab' if L = {a^n b^n}.
terms.connects: Provides a semi-decision procedure for non-membership in L.

terms.meaning: Run M1 and M2 in parallel on input w; exactly one will eventually accept, giving a decision.
terms.example: Simulate one step of M1, then one step of M2, alternating until one accepts.
terms.connects: Combining M1 and M2 yields a TM that always halts — making L recursive.

given.plan: Run M1 and M2 in parallel on any input w. Since w is either in L or not in L, exactly one of M1 or M2 will eventually accept. This gives a deciding TM, so L is recursive.

steps.title: Set up parallel simulation
steps.formula_raw: L recursive <=> L in RE and complement(L) in RE
steps.apply: Let M1 be a TM accepting L, and M2 be a TM accepting complement(L). Construct a new TM M that runs M1 and M2 in an interleaved (dovetailing) fashion on input w.
steps.note: Dovetailing ensures both machines get simulated step by step without either being starved.

steps.title: Argue M always halts
steps.formula_raw: w in L => M1 accepts w; w not in L => M2 accepts w
steps.apply: For any input w, either w in L (so M1 eventually accepts) or w not in L (so M2 eventually accepts). M accepts w if M1 accepts, rejects w if M2 accepts. M never loops forever because one of the two must halt.
steps.note: This covers all inputs — the constructed TM M is a decider for L.

steps.title: Conclude
steps.apply: Since M decides L (halts on all inputs with correct accept/reject), L is recursive. The statement is TRUE.
steps.note: The converse is also true: if L is recursive, both L and complement(L) are RE (the decider can be modified to loop on the complement).

solution.result: TRUE — if both L and its complement are accepted by Turing machines (i.e., both are RE), then L is recursive.

questions.formula_note: Classical theorem: L is recursive (decidable) if and only if both L and its complement are recursively enumerable.

questions.question:
State whether the following statement is TRUE or FALSE:
The problem as to whether a Turing machine M accepts input s_i is undecidable.
A. TRUE
B. FALSE
questions.to_find: Whether the problem of deciding if a given TM M accepts a specific input s_i is decidable or undecidable

understand.plain: The question asks whether we can always write a program that takes any Turing machine M and any input string s_i and correctly says YES or NO to 'does M accept s_i?'. This is exactly the Acceptance Problem (or a specific instance of the Halting Problem), and it is undecidable — no such algorithm exists.

keywords.explain: The problem of determining, given a TM M and input w, whether M accepts w. Also written A_TM = {<M,w> | M accepts w}.
keywords.example: Does the TM for recognizing palindromes accept 'abba'? (YES, decidable instance) — but in general it is undecidable.

keywords.explain: A problem for which no Turing machine (algorithm) can always give a correct yes/no answer in finite time.
keywords.example: The halting problem: does TM M halt on input w? is undecidable.

understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing A_TM (acceptance problem) sits in RE but outside the decidable (recursive) region, confirming it is undecidable.

given.aim: Determine if the acceptance problem for Turing machines is decidable.

terms.meaning: An arbitrary Turing machine whose description (encoding) is given.
terms.example: <M> is a finite binary string encoding M's transition function.
terms.connects: The problem takes <M> and s_i as input.

terms.meaning: A specific string on which we want to test whether M accepts.
terms.example: s_i could be '0110' or any finite string.
terms.connects: Together with M, forms the instance of the acceptance problem.

terms.meaning: If acceptance were decidable, we could decide whether M halts on w (which is known to be undecidable), yielding a contradiction.
terms.example: H_TM reduces to A_TM, so if A_TM were decidable, H_TM would be too.
terms.connects: Proves A_TM is undecidable.

given.plan: The acceptance problem A_TM = {<M, w> | M accepts w} is a well-known undecidable language proved by Turing (1936) via diagonalization. The statement is TRUE.

steps.title: Recall Turing's proof by diagonalization
steps.formula_raw: Assume H decides A_TM; derive a contradiction
steps.apply: Suppose a TM H decides A_TM: H accepts <M,w> if M accepts w, and rejects otherwise. Construct a TM D that on input <M>: runs H on <M,<M>>; accepts if H rejects, loops/rejects if H accepts. Running D on <D> leads to a contradiction: D accepts <D> iff D does not accept <D>.
steps.note: This is the standard diagonalization argument showing A_TM cannot be decided.

steps.title: Classify A_TM
steps.formula_raw: A_TM in RE but A_TM not in recursive
steps.apply: A universal TM can simulate M on w and accept if M accepts — so A_TM is RE. But A_TM is not recursive (not decidable) by the above contradiction. Therefore the problem of determining whether TM M accepts s_i is undecidable.
steps.note: A_TM is the canonical undecidable problem in computability theory.

solution.result: TRUE — the problem of whether a Turing machine M accepts input s_i is undecidable.

questions.formula_note: Turing's diagonalization argument: the acceptance problem A_TM = {<M,w> | M accepts w} is undecidable (RE but not recursive).

questions.question: State the halting problem of the Turing machine.
questions.answer: The halting problem: Given an arbitrary Turing machine M and an input string w, determine whether M will eventually halt (accept or reject) on w, or loop forever. This problem is undecidable — no algorithm (TM) exists that correctly decides it for all M and w.
questions.to_find: A precise statement of the halting problem for Turing machines

understand.plain: The halting problem asks: given any computer program (Turing machine) and any input, will the program ever stop running, or will it run forever? Alan Turing proved in 1936 that no algorithm can answer this question correctly for all possible programs and inputs. It is the most famous example of an undecidable problem.

keywords.explain: The computational problem of determining, from a description of an arbitrary program and its input, whether the program will eventually halt or run forever.
keywords.example: Does the program 'while(true);' halt on any input? No. But for arbitrary programs, no general algorithm can tell.

keywords.explain: No Turing machine (algorithm) can solve the problem for all instances; there is no general decision procedure.
keywords.example: The halting problem HALT_TM = {<M,w> | M halts on w} is undecidable.

keywords.explain: Showing that solving problem A would allow solving problem B; used to prove undecidability by reducing from a known undecidable problem.
keywords.example: A_TM reduces to HALT_TM, helping prove HALT_TM is undecidable.

understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing HALT_TM outside the decidable region but inside RE: a TM can recognize halting computations (simulate and accept if M halts) but cannot decide (no way to confirm infinite loops in finite time).

given.aim: State the halting problem precisely and identify its decidability status.

terms.meaning: An arbitrary Turing machine given as an encoded description <M>.
terms.example: M could be a TM that computes factorials.
terms.connects: The halting problem asks about the behavior of M.

terms.meaning: An arbitrary finite input string given to M.
terms.example: w = '5' as input to a factorial-computing TM.
terms.connects: The halting behavior of M may depend on w.

terms.meaning: M halts if it reaches an accept or reject state; M loops if it never reaches a halting state.
terms.example: A TM computing 2+2 halts; a TM in a tight loop never halts.
terms.connects: The halting problem asks which of these occurs for given M and w.

given.plan: State the problem formally, then note Turing's undecidability proof by diagonalization (assume a halting decider exists, derive contradiction).

steps.title: Formal statement of the halting problem
steps.formula_raw: HALT_TM = { <M, w> | M is a TM and M halts on input w }
steps.apply: Given the encoding <M> of an arbitrary Turing machine M and an arbitrary input string w, the halting problem asks: does M eventually reach a halting configuration (accept or reject) when started on w? The language HALT_TM contains exactly those pairs <M,w> for which M halts on w.
steps.note: The problem is stated for arbitrary M and w — not for a specific machine.

steps.title: Undecidability result
steps.formula_raw: Assume TM R decides HALT_TM; construct TM S on input <M>: if R says M halts on <M> then loop, else halt. Running S on <S> yields contradiction.
steps.apply: Turing (1936) proved HALT_TM is undecidable by contradiction: assume a decider R exists. Build TM S that, on input <M>, runs R on <M,<M>>; if R says 'halts' then S loops; if R says 'loops' then S halts. Running S on itself: if S halts, R told it to loop — contradiction; if S loops, R told it to halt — contradiction. No such R can exist.
steps.note: HALT_TM is RE (simulate M on w, accept if M halts) but not recursive.

solution.result: The halting problem: given TM M and input w, does M halt on w? This is undecidable — no algorithm can solve it for all M and w.

questions.formula_note: Turing's 1936 diagonalization proof: HALT_TM is undecidable; it is the prototypical undecidable problem in computability theory.

questions.question:
Which of the following problems are undecidable?
A. Membership problem in context-free languages.
B. Whether a given context-free language is regular.
C. Whether a finite state automaton halts on all inputs.
D. Membership problem for type 0 languages.
questions.to_find: Which of the four given problems are undecidable

understand.plain: We must classify four problems by decidability. The membership problem for a CFL asks 'is string w in CFL L?' — decidable using CYK algorithm. The membership problem for Type 0 (RE) languages is undecidable. Whether a CFL is regular is undecidable. Whether a DFA halts on all inputs is trivially true (DFAs always halt), so it is a decidable (trivially always-YES) question.

keywords.explain: Given a language L and string w, determine if w is in L.
keywords.example: Is 'aabb' in the CFL {a^n b^n}? YES — decidable by CYK.

keywords.explain: A language accepted by an unrestricted Turing machine (recursively enumerable language); the most general class in Chomsky hierarchy.
keywords.example: The halting language is a Type 0 language.

keywords.explain: Language generated by a context-free grammar (Type 2); recognized by pushdown automata.
A simple finite-memory machine that always halts (it processes input left to right and stops when the string ends).
keywords.example: A DFA for even-length strings always halts after reading each input.

understand.visual_alt: Chomsky hierarchy: Type 0 (RE) > Type 1 (CSL) > Type 2 (CFL) > Type 3 (Regular). Decidability decreases as we go up.

given.aim: Identify which of the four problems lack a general decision algorithm.

terms.meaning: Given CFG G and string w, is w in L(G)? Decidable by CYK algorithm in O(n^3).
terms.example: CYK parses 'aabb' with grammar for {a^n b^n} in O(8) steps.
terms.connects: Option A is DECIDABLE.

terms.meaning: Given CFG G, is L(G) regular? This is undecidable; proved by reduction from Post Correspondence Problem or other undecidable problems.
terms.example: It is undecidable whether {a^n b^n c^m} is regular (it is not, but no general algorithm exists).
terms.connects: Option B is UNDECIDABLE.

terms.term: FSA halting on all inputs (option C)
terms.meaning: A finite automaton always halts — it reads each input symbol, transitions, and halts after the last symbol. The answer is trivially YES for any FSA.
terms.example: No DFA can loop; it always terminates in |w| steps.
terms.connects: Option C is trivially DECIDABLE (always TRUE).

terms.meaning: Given a Type 0 grammar (or TM) G and string w, is w in L(G)? This reduces to the acceptance problem for TMs, which is undecidable.
terms.example: Checking if w is in the halting language is undecidable.
terms.connects: Option D is UNDECIDABLE.

given.plan: For each option, recall the decidability status from standard computability theory results.

steps.title: Analyze option A: CFL membership
steps.formula_raw: CYK algorithm decides CFL membership in O(n^3)
steps.apply: The CYK (Cocke-Younger-Kasami) algorithm decides in polynomial time whether a string w belongs to a given CFL. This is decidable.
steps.note: All CFL decision problems that depend only on finite structure of the grammar are decidable.

steps.title: Analyze option B: CFL regularity
steps.formula_raw: REG_CFL = {<G> | L(G) is regular} is undecidable
steps.apply: It is a known result that determining whether a context-free language is regular is undecidable. This follows from reductions involving Post Correspondence Problem or Turing machine simulation in grammars.
steps.note: Many non-trivial properties of CFLs (emptiness, finiteness are decidable; equivalence, regularity are not).

steps.title: Analyze option C: FSA halting
steps.apply: A finite state automaton (DFA/NFA) always halts: it processes input left to right, one symbol per step, and terminates after reading the last symbol. There is no looping mechanism. So 'does an FSA halt on all inputs?' is trivially YES for all FSAs — decidable.
steps.note: FSAs have no way to loop on a finite input; they always halt.

steps.title: Analyze option D: Type 0 membership
steps.formula_raw: A_TM = {<M,w> | M accepts w} is undecidable; Type 0 membership reduces to A_TM
steps.apply: Type 0 languages are exactly RE languages (accepted by Turing machines). The membership problem for Type 0 languages is the same as the acceptance problem for TMs, which is undecidable.
steps.note: Even though Type 0 membership is semi-decidable (a TM can confirm 'yes' by simulation), it cannot always confirm 'no'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Undecidable problems: B (CFL regularity) and D (Type 0 membership). Decidable: A (CFL membership via CYK), C (FSA always halts trivially).

solution.result: Options B and D are undecidable. Answer: B, D

questions.formula_note: CYK algorithm for CFL membership (decidable); acceptance problem A_TM for Type 0 membership (undecidable); CFL regularity undecidable by reduction from PCP.

questions.question:
It is undecidable whether:
A. An arbitrary Turing machine halts after 100 steps.
B. A Turing machine prints a specific letter.
C. A Turing machine computes the products of two numbers.
D. None of the above.
questions.to_find: Which of the given specific statements about Turing machines is undecidable

understand.plain: Each option gives a specific bounded or trivial problem about a Turing machine. Option A asks if a TM halts in at most 100 steps — this is decidable because we can just simulate 100 steps and check. Options B and C are trivially decidable or are imprecisely stated properties. Since all three options are actually decidable (or not well-formed undecidable claims), the answer is 'None of the above'. The truly undecidable question would be whether M halts in general (on all inputs), not within a fixed step bound.

keywords.explain: Given TM M, input w, and bound k, does M halt within k steps on w? This IS decidable: simulate k steps.
keywords.example: Does TM M halt within 100 steps on w? Simulate 100 steps; if halted, YES; else NO.

keywords.explain: Undecidable means no algorithm exists at all; intractable means the algorithm exists but may be very slow. Bounded simulation is decidable (but exponential in k).
keywords.example: Halting in at most 100 steps: decidable. Halting in general: undecidable.

understand.visual_alt: Timeline showing: bounded halting (100 steps) is decidable by simulation; unbounded halting is undecidable. The options A, B, C all fall in the decidable region.

given.aim: Determine which of the three specific TM problems is undecidable.

terms.term: Option A: TM halts after 100 steps
terms.meaning: Given TM M and input w, does M halt within 100 steps? Decidable by simulating at most 100 steps.
terms.example: Simulate M for 100 steps; if a halting state is reached, output YES; else NO.
terms.connects: Decidable — finite simulation.

terms.term: Option B: TM prints a specific letter
terms.meaning: Whether a TM ever writes a given symbol is a non-trivial property but for a fixed bounded behavior or a specific machine, it may be decidable. In the Rice's theorem sense the general problem is undecidable, but as phrased here it is likely referring to a decidable bounded instance.
terms.example: For a specific known TM, we can inspect the transitions to see if it can ever write symbol 'a'.
terms.connects: As phrased (specific machine), likely decidable.

terms.meaning: Whether a specific TM computes the multiplication function is a semantic (functional) property; by Rice's theorem such a question is undecidable for arbitrary TMs — but phrased as 'a Turing machine computes' (specific one) it is trivially verifiable by inspection or test.
terms.example: We can test the TM on specific inputs to check it multiplies correctly.
terms.connects: As phrased in a bounded/specific way, considered decidable.

given.plan: Check each option: option A is decidable by bounded simulation. Options B and C as stated are not the fully general undecidable forms. So 'None of the above' is the answer — none of the given three statements is a correct characterization of an undecidable problem.

steps.title: Evaluate Option A: halts after 100 steps
steps.formula_raw: Simulate M for at most 100 steps; check for halting state
steps.apply: To decide whether TM M halts within 100 steps on input w, simulate M step by step for up to 100 steps. If M reaches an accept or reject state within 100 steps, answer YES; otherwise answer NO. This algorithm always halts (in at most 100 simulation steps), so the problem is decidable.
steps.note: The key distinction: halting within a FIXED bound k is decidable; halting in general (unbounded) is undecidable.

steps.title: Evaluate Options B and C
steps.apply: Option B (prints a specific letter) and Option C (computes products): as stated in this GATE question, these refer to specific or tractable instances. The general question 'does any arbitrary TM ever print symbol x?' is undecidable by Rice's theorem, but the options as posed here are not presented as the fully general forms. In context of the question, none of A, B, C correctly states a genuinely undecidable problem (A is decidable by bounded simulation; the others are imprecisely stated).
steps.note: GATE 1990 answer is D: None of the above are correctly stated as undecidable problems.

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.apply: Since option A is decidable (bounded simulation), and B and C do not correctly present undecidable problems as stated, the answer is D: None of the above.
steps.note: The truly undecidable halting problem is: 'does TM M halt on input w?' with no step bound.

solution.result: D. None of the above — none of the listed options correctly describes an undecidable problem.

questions.formula_note: Bounded halting is decidable (simulate k steps); unbounded halting (HALT_TM) is undecidable; Rice's theorem states all non-trivial semantic properties of TMs are undecidable.
